item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article, an image of a Utah salt flat, has been removed as no source found indicates the image is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There has got to be some way to use 184 (and possibly 1056 and 914) to expand a box large enough to accommodate 2700 but still small enough to fit through 2400.

Step 1: while 2400 is open, build a "tunnel" that snakes through the aperture and has lockable doors at both ends. Both entrances must be large enough to accommodate 2700.

Step 2: throw 184 into the tunnel and shut both doors. *cross fingers this does not activate 2400's anomalous time acceleration*

Step 3: let 184 do its thing long enough for the tunnel to be expanded wide enough to convey 2700 through the tunnel.

Step 4: put 1056 into 914 on "fine" or "very fine" to hopefully fix the transcription problem

Step 5: use the improved 1056 on 2700 to shrink it, and allow it to pass through to 2400-A.

Step 6: disassemble the tunnel to allow the original door for 2400 to be shut, then shut the door.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description suggests the planet is tidelocked with the portal located on the equator.

It could be the interior of a Ptolemaic crystal sphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Sanyo SCP-2400 … the colors on the internal display appear washed out.

Indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this skip. It's a good sequel to SCP-2700, and it's awesome. I want to see some of the exploration attempts, though. Maybe there's an opportunity for fridge horror there? Who knows?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, thanks to relativity it is important to note that the rate is relative to Earth, because there is no absolute universal frame of reference for time.(That's part of the whole "aether theory" that relativity threw out the window.) There is no "outside"; only various types of "inside" that can be compared to one another to determine relative frames of reference.

For a couple working examples:

  • there's a really cool experiment where they put two atomic clocks on a pair of airliners, and flew one west and the other east completely around the Earth, and landed back at the same airport. Just that one circumnavigation at a few hundred mph was enough to throw the clocks out of sync from each other by about 300 nanoseconds.
  • Getting even faster, GPS satellites orbit at about 14,000 km/hour, and they actually have to do running corrections to keep their clocks synced with clocks on Earth. (Note that accurate GPS calculations involve time differences of less than 100 nanoseconds, so even minute drift could throw off the entire system in a few hours)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have they checked the sky for stars or maybe try to send something out there? It would suck if we found out this was actually a giant planet. It would be cool if the place turns out to be on the inside skin of a sphere so large that you couldn't see it's curvature or for that matter the surface behind the sun.

Also, if we assume it's a flat plane but it turns out to be slightly convex or concave, we may be ever so slightly off on our measurements and thus fail to properly neutralize 2700. It needs to be perfectly exact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://www.cnet.com/products/sanyo-scp-2400/review/

Really a timeless phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: can we replace the word "Earth" with something pertaining to the outside? The earth isn't an isolated temporal body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. While the Empty Room pssychology does make the region of nothingness where time passes weirdly somewhat creepy, but in the end I cannot help but think: it was a lot of buildup for a good but disproportionately good last section. I love the attached log, but this still feels like it's somewhat anticlimatic even if it did eventually lead to that awesome conclusion. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was never fully satisfied with the way this article had ended, so I've gone back an added a new supplement. Enjoy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems really interesting in concept, but the article really is just very anticlimactic.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one (Xyank is quickly becoming one of my favorite characters - sorry, Veteran Staff characters ;P ).

My only issue is how each section is in a collapsible. Now, I've only read a few, but I personally think it's better to put all the Thaumiel stuff in a single collapsible at the bottom, which is what I've seen with most of the Thaumiels I've read so far (again, admittedly not a lot, but more than a few). It just kinda breaks the flow for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much any format tweak is "authorized" if you accrue the necessary upvotes to keep it on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's always been "authorised" in that we've never had to tell people to not use it.

~*~The power was inside you all along~*~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does that mean the Thaumiel class is authorised for SCPs now ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While we do have other infinite planes, I think this one is unique enough to stand on its own, with or without the connection to SCP 2700. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this. The whole thing is painfully anticlimactic as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda neat. I do dig the line "Little miracles, huh?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Xyank is mentioned in several other works besides 1780.

Charles is the son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I was really sleepy at the time, but always from SCP-1780:

No person by that name appears in the Foundation employee database […]

So, yeah, that still kinda confuses me.

Plus, is Charles Anborough the father or the son?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article does not mention the Temporal Anomalies Department, it just mentions a "Temporal Anomalies Adviser," and Xyank very much does exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's because I'm too sleepy and I argued with one of my favourite cousins, so that's why I don't get it, but why does it mention the Temporal Anomalies Department and Dr. Xiank, when SCP-1780 says that these don't exist? Is this, like, an alternate-universe Foundation document, or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is somewhat neat, I'm bothered that it depends so much on the connection to 2700 to be interesting. Everything prior to 2700 was pretty boring for me and I would've downvoted if that's where it ended. No vote as is. If the 2700 involvement was a jumping point for some further narrative, instead of the hook and the end, I would've liked that more.

Also not a fan of Thaumiel and your use of collapsibles in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, kinda like the Hyperbolic Time Chamber, except not.

Yeah.

Also, I don't really see what the first collapsible and the Thaumiel rating are for.

It really depends on how you define "Thaumiel." My definition is: The object is actively used for the containment of other anomalies, and containment procedures must be carried out while maintaining secrecy from the bulk of the Foundation itself.

Most of the Foundation is completely unaware of Site-64T's existence, or the fact that the Foundation is taking advantage of SCP-2400's temporal anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, kinda like the Hyperbolic Time Chamber, except not.

Also, I don't really see what the first collapsible and the Thaumiel rating are for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All hail the Thaumiel!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do believe that we've entered the age of Thaumiel.

Whether we wanted to or not.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you to FortuneFavor'sBold for permission to use Thad Xyank.
